findings.title: Entire FLUX contribution has no code (training, interventions, RIEBench)
findings.claim: Every pipeline, hook, and script in the repo targets SDXL Turbo only; there is no FLUX pipeline class, no FLUX SAE training, and no FLUX intervention code (grep for 'flux'/'schnell'/'FluxPipeline'/'layer 18' over all .py files and notebook code cells returns zero matches; only SDXL Turbo is loaded).
findings.concern: FLUX generalization is a headline contribution ('we consider this a crucial result', Sec. 1.1; Fig. 1 row 4; Figs 8, 9, 35-43; App. C; Fig. 14), but none of the FLUX results can be reproduced or inspected from this repository.
findings.resolution: Authors: please release the FLUX SAE-training and intervention code (App. C says k=20, nf=12288, layer-18 activations of Flux-schnell) or point to the exact repository/commit that produces Figs 1(row4), 8, 9, 14, 35-43.
findings.paper_ref: Sec. 1.1 'Additionally, we train SAEs ... FLUX Schnell'; Appendix C
findings.reason: Line 29 of collect_latents_dataset.py matches the quoted SDXL-Turbo load exactly, confirming no FLUX pipeline is present in the repo.

findings.title: No code computes Fig. 5 / Fig. 6 RIEBench metrics (LPIPS, CLIP-sim, SAM2, selection)
findings.quote:
diffusers==0.29.2
gradio==4.44.1
torch>=2.4.0
numpy
matplotlib
pillow
wandb
einops
transformers
accelerate
huggingface_hub
git+https://github.com/wendlerc/clip-retrieval.git
findings.claim: No file in the repo computes the LPIPS distance, the CLIP-similarity increase, the grounded-SAM2 segmentation masks, the eq.(8)/(9) feature-selection scores, or the PIEBench-derived edit-category harness that produce Fig. 5 and Fig. 6; correspondingly lpips/sam2/groundingdino are not even declared as dependencies.
findings.concern: Fig. 5 and Fig. 6 carry the paper's central quantitative claim (SAE features match neuron baselines 'while requiring several orders of magnitude fewer features' and reveal block specialization); none of these numbers can be reproduced from this repository.
findings.resolution: Authors: please release the RIEBench evaluation harness (the companion repo wendlerc/RIEBench appears to be it) and pin it from this repo, including LPIPS/CLIP-score computation and the SAM2 mask generation, so Fig. 5/6 are reproducible.
findings.reason: requirements.txt lines 1-12 match the quoted content exactly and confirm lpips/sam2/groundingdino are absent as dependencies.

findings.title: No driver produces Fig. 2 (EV vs k / expansion) or Fig. 3 (EV across steps, feature overlap)
findings.quote:
def explained_variance(recons, x):
    # Compute the variance of the difference
    diff = x - recons
    diff_var = torch.var(diff, dim=0, unbiased=False)

    # Compute the variance of the original tensor
    x_var = torch.var(x, dim=0, unbiased=False)

    # Avoid division by zero
    explained_var = 1 - diff_var / (x_var + 1e-8)

    return explained_var.mean()
findings.claim: The repo defines a per-batch explained_variance() that is logged to wandb during training, but there is no script that (a) sweeps k and expansion factor and aggregates EV for Fig. 2, or (b) runs the SAEs over 4-step/20-step SDXL generations and computes EV per denoising step plus the adjacent-timestep cosine-similarity 'feature overlap' for Fig. 3 (no cosine_similarity / overlap code exists).
findings.concern: Fig. 2 and Fig. 3 (including the headline 'one-step is enough' generalization-across-steps result) cannot be regenerated; only a training-time scalar EV exists, not the plotted sweep or multi-step curves.
findings.resolution: Authors: please release the evaluation notebook/script that computes the Fig. 2 sweep and the Fig. 3 multi-step EV and feature-overlap curves.
findings.reason: train_sae.py lines 227-238 contain only the per-batch explained_variance function as quoted; no sweep driver or multi-step EV evaluation code is present in the repo.

findings.title: Table 5 reconstruction numbers have a hook primitive but no driver to compute them
findings.claim: reconstruct_sae_hook is defined but never called by any script or notebook; nothing computes the pixel Manhattan mean/median distance, the LPIPS column, or the per-block EV (%) over 100 prompts that populate Table 5 (24 rows × 4 columns).
findings.concern: The Table 5 reconstruction-quality numbers (and Fig. 18) cannot be reproduced from the repo; only the intervention primitive exists.
findings.resolution: Authors: please release the script that drives reconstruct_sae_hook over 100 LAION-COCO prompts and computes the Manhattan/LPIPS/EV columns of Table 5.
findings.reason: utils/hooks.py lines 110-115 match the quoted reconstruct_sae_hook definition exactly, confirming the primitive exists but no driver or LPIPS/Manhattan computation is present.

findings.title: Table 1 per-block ablation LPIPS has a hook primitive but no driver/LPIPS computation
findings.claim: ablate_block (returns the block input, i.e. zeroes the residual update) is defined but never called by any script or notebook; nothing iterates over the 28 blocks, ablates each, generates 20 random prompts, and computes the mean LPIPS distance reported in Table 1.
findings.concern: Table 1 (the rebuttal-added quantitative causal-impact ranking of resnet/attention blocks) cannot be reproduced from the repo.
findings.resolution: Authors: please release the script that drives ablate_block per block over 20 prompts and computes the mean LPIPS in Table 1.
findings.reason: utils/hooks.py lines 118-120 match the quoted ablate_block definition exactly; the function is a stub with no driver calling it.

findings.title: example.ipynb imports add_feature_on_area / replace_with_feature, which do not exist
findings.claim: The first code cell of example.ipynb runs `from utils import add_feature_on_area, replace_with_feature`, but utils/hooks.py (re-exported via `from .hooks import *`) defines only add_feature_on_area_base/_base_both/_base_cond/_turbo and replace_with_feature_base/_turbo — there is no symbol named add_feature_on_area or replace_with_feature.
findings.concern: The README directs users to example.ipynb for analysis examples, yet its very first cell raises ImportError, so the documented usage example does not run as shipped.
findings.resolution: Rename the imports in example.ipynb to the *_turbo (or *_base) variants, or add aliases add_feature_on_area / replace_with_feature in utils/hooks.py.
findings.reason: Confirmed: example.ipynb cell 0 imports bare `add_feature_on_area` and `replace_with_feature`, and utils/hooks.py (via `from .hooks import *`) defines only the `_base`/`_turbo` variants, so ImportError is real.

findings.title: SAETrainingConfig.save_path references free name save_path_base, not self.save_path_base
findings.claim: save_path_base is stored as an instance field of SAETrainingConfig (line 12) yet the save_path property references the bare name save_path_base instead of self.save_path_base; there is no module-level or builtin save_path_base, so evaluating cfg.save_path raises NameError.
findings.concern: train_sae.py calls cfg.save_path at every save_interval (line 187) and at the end of training (line 204), so a training run crashes with NameError the first time it tries to checkpoint, preventing the SAEs from being saved.
findings.resolution: Change `save_path_base` to `self.save_path_base` in the save_path property of SAETrainingConfig.
findings.reason: SAE/sae_utils.py lines 23-25 confirm the save_path property uses bare `save_path_base` while line 12 stores it as the instance field `self.save_path_base`, guaranteeing a NameError at runtime.

findings.title: Code trains on guangyil/laion-coco-aesthetic, paper cites LAION-COCO [54]
findings.claim: The latent-collection script streams prompts from the HuggingFace dataset `guangyil/laion-coco-aesthetic` (an aesthetic-filtered third-party re-host), whereas the paper states the SAEs were trained on '1.5M prompts from the LAION-COCO [54]', citing the original LAION blog dataset (laion.ai/blog/laion-coco).
findings.concern: The actual training prompts come from an aesthetic-filtered subset, not the original LAION-COCO captioning set the paper cites; both are valid prompt sources, but the description and the artefact differ.
findings.resolution: Authors: clarify whether the reported SAEs were trained on guangyil/laion-coco-aesthetic and update the citation, or provide the script variant that uses the original LAION-COCO.
findings.reason: collect_latents_dataset.py line 28 matches the quoted dataset load exactly, confirming the aesthetic-filtered variant is used rather than the cited original LAION-COCO.

findings.title: Shipped SAE/config.json defaults to k=20, paper's main SDXL SAEs use k=10
findings.claim: The training config shipped in the repo sets k=20 for both SAEs, while the paper's main SDXL Turbo SAEs (and the provided checkpoints) use k=10 (Appendix K: 'The value of k is set to 10'); the checkpoint folders are k10_hidden5120.
findings.concern: A user running the documented `python scripts/train_sae.py` reproduces a k=20 SAE, not the paper's main k=10 SAE; this is a template/default mismatch rather than a result error since the released checkpoints are correctly k=10.
findings.resolution: Authors: set the example config k to 10 (and document the k/expansion sweep used for Fig. 2), or note that config.json is only a template.
findings.paper_ref: Appendix K 'The value of k is set to 10'
findings.reason: SAE/config.json lines 2-12 match the quoted content exactly, confirming both configs ship with k=20 rather than the paper's k=10.
